content: YAGNI literature is explicit: building a generic plugin system 'in case we need it later' when the app needs one fixed behavior is the canonical YAGNI violation — implement the single behavior now, add plugin architecture only when a SECOND plugin actually exists (rule of three). Python plugin discovery has three flavors (naming convention, namespace packages, package-metadata entry_points) plus runtime importlib scanning; libs like stevedore/yapsy exist but are framework-scale. For a single-user internal tool, entry-point/dynamic discovery is over-engineering: a static registry dict (import-time, in-repo) gives the same extensibility with zero discovery machinery, no setup.py entry-points, no import-order hazards. Unused extensibility adds complexity, maintenance burden, and bugs.
